slides.title: Putting the Equipment Where It Counts
slides.subtitle: Detecting Attacks: how to place cameras, motion sensors, locks, and guards so that detection controls actually catch attacks instead of crying wolf
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Welcome back to Lesson 2.4, Day 2 of 4. Yesterday, 2.4.A, you learned the four ways security controls detect a physical attack — cameras, guards, motion sensors, and employees. But a detection control is only as good as where you put it. Today is 2.4.B: determining the effective placement of detection and monitoring equipment. We'll cover what to consider when placing cameras — coverage, angle, tamper-resistance, and ingress and egress points; where motion sensors belong and where they create so many false alarms they become useless; where locks and access control vestibules go; and the difference between stationary and patrolling guards. The recurring idea today is that placement is what turns equipment into actual detection — the same sensor is invaluable in a server room and worthless in a busy lobby.

slides.prompt: You have exactly one motion sensor to install in a lab. Two candidate spots: (A) the main lobby, where dozens of employees and visitors walk past every hour all day; (B) the server room, which is normally empty and only two people are authorized to enter.
slides.subprompt: Write 3–5 sentences: where do you install the sensor, and why? What would go wrong if you put it in the OTHER location? Use the word 'false alarm' in your answer.
slides.directions: Independent writing. I will cold-call 2–3 students.
slides.script: Two minutes, on your own. You have one motion sensor and two spots: the busy main lobby, or the normally-empty server room only two people may enter. Where does it go, and why? And what specifically goes wrong if you put it in the other spot? I want the phrase 'false alarm' in your answer. Hold it. This is EK 2.4.B.2 almost word for word — the College Board says motion sensors belong where traffic is unexpected, not where everyone walks, and the reason is exactly the false-alarm problem you're about to write about.

slides.heading: What You’ll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Day 2 Guided Notes have three sections that match today’s lesson. Green = CB required. Blue = enrichment.

sections.title: Placing Cameras Effectively
sections.description: Coverage, angle, tamper-resistance, what the camera could capture an adversary doing, and monitoring points of ingress and egress (EK 2.4.B.1).

sections.title: Sensors, Locks/Vestibules, and Guards
sections.description: Motion sensors where traffic is unexpected, locks and access control vestibules on sensitive areas, and stationary vs. patrolling guards (EK 2.4.B.2, B.3, B.4).

sections.title: Enrichment: The False-Alarm Trap
sections.description: Why a sensor in a high-traffic area is worse than none — too many false alarms train people to ignore the real one (EK 2.4.B.2).

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Lesson 2.4 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Three sections today, all green except the last. Section one is placing cameras: coverage, angle, the risk of tampering, what a camera in a given spot could actually capture an adversary doing, and why ingress and egress points get camera coverage. Section two covers the other three controls' placement: motion sensors where traffic is unexpected, locks and access control vestibules on sensitive areas, and stationary versus patrolling guards. Section three is a quick enrichment on the false-alarm trap — why a badly placed sensor is worse than no sensor at all. Keep your pencil moving; I collect Guided Notes at the end of class.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Place a camera using the four considerations — coverage, angle, tamper-resistance, and what it could capture — and cover points of ingress and egress (LO 2.4.B)
Place a motion sensor where traffic is unexpected and explain why high-traffic placement breeds false alarms that get the alarm ignored (LO 2.4.B)
Choose locks/vestibules for sensitive entries and decide stationary (chokepoints) vs. patrolling (perimeters) guards, justifying each (LO 2.4.B)

slides.script: Three objectives today, all under learning objective 2.4.B — determine effective placement of security controls. Notice the verb changed from yesterday: yesterday was identify the methods, today is determine where each one goes. First, place a camera using the four considerations the CED names — coverage, angle, tamper-resistance, and what the camera could capture an adversary doing — and remember points of ingress and egress are often monitored by camera. Second, place a motion sensor where traffic is unexpected, and explain why putting one in a high-traffic area breeds false alarms that train people to ignore the alarm. Third, choose locks and access control vestibules for sensitive entries, and decide whether a guard should be stationary at a chokepoint or patrolling a perimeter — justifying each with the CED's reasoning. The exam will give you a space and ask where a control should go and why.

slides.label: Placing Cameras Effectively
slides.script: Section two of the lesson. Learning objective 2.4.B. Start with cameras, because their placement has the most moving parts — coverage, angle, tampering, and what the shot actually captures. Ten minutes here.

slides.heading: Where Cameras Go: Coverage, Angle, Tamper, Purpose
slides.subheading: EK 2.4.B.1 — placement is not 'point it at the room'; it's four deliberate considerations.

left.title: Four Placement Considerations (EK 2.4.B.1)
left.definition: What to weigh before mounting a camera
examples: Visual coverage — does the camera see the area that matters?
Angle — is the view useful, or does it miss faces and actions?
Ability to be tampered with — can an adversary block, turn, or disable it?
What it could capture — what would an adversary be doing here, and how would that footage help?

right.title: Ingress and Egress (EK 2.4.B.1)
examples: Points of ingress and egress are often monitored by camera
Ingress = ways in; egress = ways out
Everyone entering or leaving passes a chokepoint — ideal for capturing identity
A camera at a doorway captures who came and went, and when

slides.footer: AP TIP: EK 2.4.B.1 names FOUR camera considerations — coverage, angle, tamper-resistance, and what the footage could capture — plus the reminder that points of ingress and egress are often monitored by camera. Be ready to apply all of them to a floor plan.
slides.script: Camera placement, EK 2.4.B.1, is four deliberate considerations, not 'point it at the room.' On the left: first, visual coverage — does it actually see the area that matters? Second, angle — a camera aimed too high catches the tops of heads and no faces; the angle has to make the footage useful. Third, the ability to be tampered with — can an adversary reach up and block it, turn it, spray it, or cut power? Mount it out of reach and protect it. Fourth, and this is the subtle one the CED calls out: think about what a camera in this specific spot could capture an adversary doing, and how that information would be helpful. On the right is the highest-value placement: points of ingress and egress — ways in and ways out — are often monitored by camera, because everyone entering or leaving funnels past a doorway, the perfect chokepoint to capture identity and timing. Write the four considerations and the ingress/egress rule.

slides.label: Sensors, Locks, and Guards
slides.script: Section three. Same objective, 2.4.B. Now the other three controls — motion sensors, locks and vestibules, and the two kinds of guards. Each has a placement rule the exam loves. Twelve minutes.

slides.heading: Placing Sensors and Guards: Three CB Rules
slides.subheading: EK 2.4.B.2, B.4 (and the false-alarm logic). Right placement makes each control work; wrong placement breaks it.

items.title: Motion Sensors → Unexpected Traffic
items.summary: Motion sensors should be placed where traffic is unexpected, like server rooms, or areas where sensitive materials are stored and few people have access — places where any movement is itself suspicious.

items.title: Why Not High-Traffic Areas
items.summary: Motion sensors in high-traffic areas create many false alarms, making the alarms less likely to be taken seriously when there is a real security event — a badly placed sensor is worse than none.

items.title: Stationary Guards → Chokepoints
items.summary: Stationary guards provide constant protection for a specific area, entrance, or high-value item, and are highly effective at places that funnel traffic — entry gates, main entrances, lobbies, and entrances to more secure areas.

slides.script: Three placement rules, EK 2.4.B.2 and B.4. First, motion sensors go where traffic is unexpected — server rooms, storage for sensitive materials, places few people may enter — because there, ANY movement is itself the alarm. Second, the flip side, and this is the one the exam tests: motion sensors in high-traffic areas create many false alarms, and those false alarms train people to ignore the alarm, so when a real event finally happens, no one takes it seriously. A sensor in the wrong place is worse than no sensor. Third, EK 2.4.B.4 on stationary guards: they give constant protection to a specific area, entrance, or high-value item, and they're highly effective at places that funnel traffic — entry gates, main entrances, lobbies, the doors to more secure areas. Write all three. We'll cover patrolling guards and locks on the next slide.

slides.heading: Locks, Vestibules, and Patrolling Guards
slides.subheading: EK 2.4.B.3 and the rest of EK 2.4.B.4 — protecting sensitive entries and covering the perimeter.

left.title: Locks & Access Control Vestibules (EK 2.4.B.3)
examples: Locks should be placed on all entries to areas with sensitive information or systems
For particularly sensitive areas, use an access control vestibule at the entry point
A vestibule prevents piggybacking or tailgating — one badge, one person through
This carries forward the locks and vestibules you met in Topic 2.3

right.title: Patrolling Guards (EK 2.4.B.4)
examples: Patrolling guards are harder for an adversary to plan around
Their unpredictable rounds create time pressure for an adversary
They are better suited for perimeters and exterior areas
Stationary guards funnel-points; patrolling guards cover ground

slides.footer: AP TIP: EK 2.4.B.4 contrasts STATIONARY (constant protection at chokepoints) with PATROLLING (unpredictable, for perimeters). EK 2.4.B.3 puts locks on every sensitive entry and a vestibule where the stakes are highest.
slides.script: Two more placements. On the left, EK 2.4.B.3, locks and vestibules: locks go on ALL entries to areas with sensitive information or systems, and for the most sensitive areas, an access control vestibule at the entry point — that's the two-door airlock from Topic 2.3 — to prevent piggybacking and tailgating, so one badge lets exactly one person through. On the right, the other half of EK 2.4.B.4, patrolling guards: unlike a stationary guard fixed at a chokepoint, a patrolling guard is harder for an adversary to plan around because their rounds are unpredictable, which creates time pressure — the adversary doesn't know when the guard returns. Patrolling guards are better for perimeters and exterior areas. So the rule of thumb: stationary guards for chokepoints that funnel traffic, patrolling guards for perimeters and open ground. Write both placements.

slides.heading: The ‘More Sensors Everywhere’ Myth
slides.misconception: The more motion sensors you install and the more places you put them, the better your detection — sensors in every hallway and lobby means nothing gets past you.
slides.whyItHappens: It feels intuitive that more coverage is always safer, and a sensor in a busy area technically does detect the movement that passes it. Students assume any detection is good detection.
slides.correction: EK 2.4.B.2 says the opposite for high-traffic areas: motion sensors there create many false alarms, which makes the alarms less likely to be taken seriously when there is a real security event. Placement, not quantity, is what makes a sensor effective. A sensor belongs where traffic is UNEXPECTED — a server room, sensitive storage — so that any movement at all is meaningful.
slides.example: Put a motion sensor in a busy lobby and it fires hundreds of times a day on employees and visitors. Within a week, security tunes it out as noise — and the one night an adversary trips it, no one responds, because they have learned the alarm means nothing. The same sensor in the empty server room would have meant 'someone is in here who shouldn't be.' Same device, opposite value, decided entirely by placement.
slides.script: This is the placement misconception the exam loves, and it's a trap because it sounds reasonable: more sensors, more coverage, more safety. But read EK 2.4.B.2. In high-traffic areas, motion sensors create many false alarms, and here's the human consequence the CED spells out — those false alarms make people stop taking the alarm seriously, so when a real event happens, nobody responds. A sensor in a busy lobby fires hundreds of times a day on ordinary employees; within a week security tunes it out as noise, and the one night an adversary trips it, the alarm gets ignored because it has always meant nothing. The exact same sensor in the empty server room would have screamed 'intruder.' Same device, opposite value, and the only difference is placement. So the rule isn't 'more sensors' — it's sensors where traffic is unexpected, so every alarm is worth answering. Write the cause: false alarms erode trust in the alarm.

slides.heading: Enrichment: Placement Turns a Device Into Detection
slides.subheading: Not a separate EK, but the unifying idea behind all of EK 2.4.B — the same equipment is strong or worthless depending on where it sits.

left.title: Well-Placed = Real Detection
examples: Camera at a doorway captures every face entering (EK 2.4.B.1)
Sensor in an empty server room flags any movement (EK 2.4.B.2)
Stationary guard at the main entrance screens all traffic (EK 2.4.B.4)
Vestibule on the vault door stops tailgating cold (EK 2.4.B.3)

right.title: Poorly Placed = False Confidence
examples: Camera angled at the ceiling captures no faces (EK 2.4.B.1)
Sensor in a busy lobby drowns in false alarms (EK 2.4.B.2)
Patrolling guard on a fixed schedule is easy to time around (EK 2.4.B.4)
A reachable camera an adversary can simply turn or cover (EK 2.4.B.1)

slides.footer: TAKEAWAY: Every EK 2.4.B rule is really one idea — placement converts a device into actual detection. The wrong location doesn't just fail; it creates false confidence, which is more dangerous than no control at all.
slides.script: Quick enrichment to tie 2.4.B together. Every placement rule in this objective is really the same idea: placement is what turns a device into detection. On the left, well-placed controls — a doorway camera that captures every face, a server-room sensor where any movement matters, a stationary guard screening the main entrance, a vestibule that stops tailgating. On the right, the identical devices placed wrong — a camera angled at the ceiling that captures no faces, a lobby sensor buried in false alarms, a patrolling guard on a predictable schedule an adversary can time, a camera mounted low enough to simply cover. And here's the danger that's worse than no control: a poorly placed control creates false confidence. You think you're protected, you stop watching, and you're actually more exposed than if you'd installed nothing. That's why 2.4.B exists as its own objective — owning the equipment isn't enough; placing it correctly is the skill.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO 2.4.B)

terms.definition: Whether a camera's placement and view actually capture the area that matters, one of the considerations when placing cameras (EK 2.4.B.1).
The ways into and out of a space — chokepoints that are often monitored by camera because everyone passes through them (EK 2.4.B.1).
A detection alert triggered by harmless activity; many of them, as from a motion sensor in a high-traffic area, make real alarms less likely to be taken seriously (EK 2.4.B.2).
A controlled entry point used for particularly sensitive areas to prevent piggybacking or tailgating, placed at the entry to such areas (EK 2.4.B.3).
A guard who provides constant protection for a specific area, entrance, or high-value item, most effective at places that funnel traffic (EK 2.4.B.4).
A guard who moves on rounds, harder for an adversary to plan around and better suited to perimeters and exterior areas (EK 2.4.B.4).

slides.script: Six terms for 2.4.B. The camera pair — visual coverage and points of ingress and egress — are the EK 2.4.B.1 ideas the exam tests, so know that ingress means ways in and egress means ways out. False alarm is the single most important term today: it's the reason motion sensors don't go in busy areas, EK 2.4.B.2. Access control vestibule carries over from Topic 2.3 and is the EK 2.4.B.3 answer for the most sensitive entries. And know the stationary-versus-patrolling guard distinction from EK 2.4.B.4 — stationary for chokepoints, patrolling for perimeters. All six, with EK numbers.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Independent Writing
slides.subheading: Both questions map to LO 2.4.B. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: A facilities manager wants to install motion sensors in the main lobby, the break room, and a rarely-used archive room holding sensitive records. Using EK 2.4.B.2, explain where the sensor belongs, where it does NOT, and exactly what goes wrong if it is placed in a high-traffic area.

You are placing a single camera and a single guard at a lab. Using EK 2.4.B.1 and EK 2.4.B.4, explain where you would place the camera (and the considerations behind it) and whether you would station the guard or have them patrol — and why.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Two questions, six minutes, independent, full sentences. Question one: motion sensors for a lobby, a break room, and a quiet archive of sensitive records — using EK 2.4.B.2, say where the sensor goes, the archive, where it must not go, the lobby and break room, and what specifically goes wrong in high-traffic spots: false alarms that get the alarm ignored. Question two: one camera, one guard — using 2.4.B.1, place the camera at a point of ingress or egress and name your considerations, coverage, angle, tamper-resistance; and using 2.4.B.4, decide stationary or patrolling and justify it. Be precise.

slides.heading: End of Day 2
todayPoints: Placed cameras using the four EK 2.4.B.1 considerations — coverage, angle, tamper-resistance, and what the footage could capture — plus monitoring points of ingress and egress
Placed motion sensors where traffic is unexpected, and saw why high-traffic placement creates many false alarms that get the alarm ignored (EK 2.4.B.2)
Placed locks on all sensitive entries and access control vestibules at the most sensitive ones to stop piggybacking and tailgating (EK 2.4.B.3)
Contrasted stationary guards at chokepoints with patrolling guards on perimeters (EK 2.4.B.4)
nextPoints: Apply all the detection techniques to Scenario 2A, Securing Xtensr Labs (LO 2.4.C)
Pair cameras with facial recognition, and motion detectors with cameras
Use badge entry logs to detect tailgating by how long a door stays open
slides.teaser: Tonight, picture the Xtensr Labs you're assessing: a lobby, a server room, a badge-locked records vault. Tomorrow you'll put everything together — applying detection techniques to find exactly how an adversary would be caught in that real building.
slides.script: Day 2 done. You can now place each detection control and justify it the way the CED does — cameras by coverage, angle, tamper-resistance, and purpose, with ingress and egress covered; motion sensors where traffic is unexpected, never in busy areas; locks on every sensitive entry and vestibules on the most sensitive; stationary guards at chokepoints, patrolling guards on perimeters. Tomorrow, Day 3, we leave the abstract and apply all of it to Scenario 2A, Securing Xtensr Labs — using detection techniques to identify physical attacks in a real building, pairing cameras with facial recognition, motion detectors with cameras, and reading badge logs to catch tailgating. Review your Guided Notes — be able to place every control and justify it with its EK number.
